principles.text: You strive to centralize your personal data to maintain persistent context across tools.
You value building personalized tools to consolidate and control your own digital life.
You strive for 'expressive adequacy' in design, enabling tools to adapt to unique user workflows.
You believe changing ingrained habits and childhood conditioning requires significant, conscious cognitive effort.
You value being understood accurately without premature assumptions about your communication or direction.
You value sharing your authentic workflows and technical projects with others through writing.
You process your thoughts, ideas, and personal growth through raw, unfiltered writing.
You seek existential clarity and systemic alignment before taking tactical action.
You value deep self-awareness and understanding the underlying drivers of your behavior.
You value rigorous intellectual honesty and constructive pushback over superficial comfort and validation.
You value creating highly interactive, innovative, and personalized digital representations of yourself.
You value flexible, unopinionated systems built from simple, composable primitives rather than rigid structures.
You value preserving human experience to combat forgetfulness and the anxiety of impermanence.
You value your self-worth and actively reject people-pleasing and self-minimization.

events.content: back home in winnipeg. want you to be realistic. got 8 days left before i leave for toronto again. how do i sleep in the living room. enjoy sleeping there because it's central (my actual room is upstairs, to desribe it, feels like the leaf node of a tree). my living room has the

Aaryan is back in Winnipeg for 8 days before heading to Toronto. He wants practical advice on sleeping in the living room comfortably. He enjoys it because it feels central/connected vs his room feeling like a "leaf node." Let me just give practical advice here - this is a simple
honestly, mainly just like bed wise. a sleeping bag?

He's asking about what to sleep on/in - sleeping bag vs other options.Sleeping bag works fine for 8 days, especially if Winnipeg nights are still cool. Only downside is you'll get hot if the living room warms up — sleeping bags are designed to trap heat, so if you're someone who
but how to clean the thing after i'm gone. don't want mom to clean it

Simple practical question.Shake it out before you leave, fold it back up, put it away yourself. That's it — you're sleeping in it, not eating in it. 8 days of sleep doesn't make a sleeping bag meaningfully dirty, just a bit sweaty. If you want to be thorough, air it out outside f
mattress? what
Fair — dragging a full mattress downstairs is more effort than 8 days warrants. Sleeping bag or your duvet/blanket from upstairs on the floor or couch is the right call then. Keep it simple.

Purpose & context
Aaryan is a UBC CS student (~3.7 GPA) approaching graduation, targeting new grad roles at frontier AI labs (Anthropic, OpenAI), ML systems companies (Groq, Cerebras, Tenstorrent, NVIDIA), and Series A/B startups. Core interests span ML systems, inference optimiz

If there was no prestige endpoint, I think I'd want to be working either for quant, because again that's the next prestige endpoint, or I'd still want to probably be doing something in ML. I think I was always interested in ML. When it started blowing up, I was actually kind of l

I want to push back on that. You asked me what I'm excited about Amazon Alexa. The team that I'm working on does not do any math or statistics, so I cannot say I'm excited about math or statistics if I know that I'm not going to be working on math and statistics.

Fair pushback. He's saying his answer was context-constrained — he was describing what excites him about a specific role that doesn't involve math/stats, so of course he didn't mention math/stats. That's a valid point and I should concede it.That's a fair point, and I'll take it

I've actually not yet been deep in systems. I wouldn't say my starter job currently has led me to be any deep in systems. I liked doing STAT 305, Statistical Inference at UBC; that was my first course. I really also liked doing STAT 302. Those were genuinely courses where I was l

This is a really good, honest answer. Let me think about what's actually being revealed here.

He's describing two genuine intellectual pleasures:
1. Statistical modeling - capturing messy real-world phenomena into mathematical structures you can examine
2. Computer systems - hav

events.content: back home in winnipeg. want you to be realistic. got 8 days left before i leave for toronto again. how do i sleep in the living room. enjoy sleeping there because it's central (my actual room is upstairs, to desribe it, feels like the leaf node of a tree). my living room has the

Aaryan is back in Winnipeg for 8 days before heading to Toronto. He wants practical advice on sleeping in the living room comfortably. He enjoys it because it feels central/connected vs his room feeling like a "leaf node." Let me just give practical advice here - this is a simple
honestly, mainly just like bed wise. a sleeping bag?

He's asking about what to sleep on/in - sleeping bag vs other options.Sleeping bag works fine for 8 days, especially if Winnipeg nights are still cool. Only downside is you'll get hot if the living room warms up — sleeping bags are designed to trap heat, so if you're someone who
but how to clean the thing after i'm gone. don't want mom to clean it

Simple practical question.Shake it out before you leave, fold it back up, put it away yourself. That's it — you're sleeping in it, not eating in it. 8 days of sleep doesn't make a sleeping bag meaningfully dirty, just a bit sweaty. If you want to be thorough, air it out outside f
mattress? what
Fair — dragging a full mattress downstairs is more effort than 8 days warrants. Sleeping bag or your duvet/blanket from upstairs on the floor or couch is the right call then. Keep it simple.
